opp.ais_why:
* The customer actively evaluates AI as a criterion: a formal RFP question asked "what are AI elements and what modules of your service are they used?" and Rocio Hinojosa "expressed a desire to understand the available AI solutions that have already been developed from a procurement perspective."
* Concrete AI benchmarking: the customer requested "AI success cases, including tail-spend analytics and autonomous negotiation examples, to benchmark platform capabilities," and discussion covered Zycus's agentic AI for sourcing/negotiation and its Anthropic Claude-based architecture.
* It reads as diligent RFP evaluation rather than excitement - this EY-led, multi-vendor process is also weighing pricing/discounts, China support and references on a $500K deal at 25%; AI is one criterion among many.

metrics.narrative: Nidec has articulated operational pain (manual procurement processes, supplier inefficiency, tail-spend visibility gaps, sourcing automation needs) across the evaluation, evidenced by the 8-product scope requested (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Certinal, iRisk, Merlin Intake per Products__c) and the RFP requirements for operational-improvement case studies with specific KPIs (supplier onboarding time, sourcing automation, cycle-time reduction metrics per Next_Step__c). However, Nidec has NOT quantified the business case: no cost savings target, headcount reduction goal, cycle-time improvement metric, or ROI threshold has been stated by the buyer across any milestone (RFI 31 Mar, Q&A 20 Apr, presentation 6 Jun, RFP 30 Jun per Next_Step__c). Manabu requested pricing justification on the 20 Apr Q&A call (Next_Step__c 13 Apr: pricing flagged as significantly higher than competitors), signaling he must defend the investment upward, but no approver has validated the business case or named a quantified success metric. The absence of a quantified value case weakens Zycus ability to anchor the pricing justification and differentiate vs the Coupa-backed finalist on ROI strength, a critical gap before the shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August.

champion.narrative: No champion has been developed. Manabu Serizawa (Decision Maker per Contact Role, Procurement Officer, serizawa.manabu@nidec.com) is the sole identified and engaged stakeholder across the 97-day deal cycle (created 26 Mar, last activity 1 Jul per SF authoritative facts). Manabu is transactional and evaluator-focused: he requested pricing justification on the 20 Apr Q&A call (Next_Step__c 13 Apr: pricing flagged as significantly higher than competitors) and led the RFP submission process (RFP due 30 Jun, submitted on time per Next_Step__c 31st), but has NOT shared competitive intelligence (second finalist identity unconfirmed, Coupa-backed finalist positioning unknown beyond Next_Step__c 31 Mar KPMG rejection), has NOT advocated for Zycus vs the Coupa-backed finalist, and has NOT introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer (Izabela Smulska Kowalczyk, Global CPO, connected and invited to Horizon as of 6 Jun per Next_Step__c but has not joined any evaluation milestone) or other C-level sponsors. Manabu's engagement is procedural compliance (RFI, Q&A, RFP milestones) rather than advocacy. No other stakeholder has demonstrated champion behaviors (pushing back on competitors for Zycus, sharing internal priorities, driving the agenda, providing coaching on how to win). Izabela Smulska Kowalczyk (Global CPO) was connected on 6 Jun and invited to Horizon (Next_Step__c) but has not participated, signaling passive or deferred engagement rather than active sponsorship. The absence of a champion leaves Zycus blind to competitive dynamics (who is the second finalist, what is the Coupa-backed finalist's wedge, what is Nidec's true decision priority) and without an internal advocate to validate the AI business case, de-risk the pricing concern, or secure executive buy-in before the shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August. Developing a champion (ideally Manabu elevated to advocacy role, or a senior procurement/IT leader engaged) is the highest-leverage move to gain competitive intelligence and internal sponsorship.

competition.narrative: Nidec shortlisted 3 vendors as of 6 Jun (Next_Step__c: strong presentation to global team in Japan, Poland, US, China, shortlisted to 3, looking to shortlist to 2). The primary competitor is a Coupa-backed finalist with KPMG as the SI partner (Next_Step__c 31 Mar: EY included Zycus in 4-vendor eval, KPMG rejected Zycus partnership inquiry as they are aligned with the relevant stakeholder). This finalist holds structural advantage: KPMG is an established SI with Nidec relationship (they declined to partner with Zycus), creating preferred-partner leverage on China pilot implementation, change management, and local support, while Zycus SI partner remains unresolved (TCS explored per Next_Step__c 31 Mar but uncommitted in 90 days). The second finalist's identity is unconfirmed (likely SAP Ariba, GEP, or Ivalua based on enterprise S2P market positioning, but no name disclosed by Nidec or EY per Next_Step__c). The fourth RFI vendor was eliminated after the 6 Jun presentations. No competitive intelligence has been shared by Manabu, EY, or any Nidec stakeholder to help Zycus understand the Coupa-backed finalist's wedge, the second finalist's strengths, or Nidec's true decision priorities (lowest-cost vs best-value, incumbent-adjacent preference). Manabu flagged Zycus pricing as significantly higher than competitors on the 20 Apr Q&A call (Next_Step__c 13 Apr), signaling pricing competitiveness is a live threat. The wedge to win: Zycus must anchor on Merlin AI differentiation (Merlin Intake in scope per Products__c, AI customer success cases requested in RFP per Next_Step__c) and quantified ROI proof (5-6 operational case studies with KPIs requested per Next_Step__c) to justify the pricing premium vs the Coupa-backed finalist, while urgently locking an SI partner (TCS or alternative) to neutralize the KPMG structural advantage before the shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August. Risk: Zycus may be in a vendor-diversity slot rather than a genuine contender if Nidec has already leaned toward the Coupa-backed finalist (incumbent-adjacent positioning, established SI relationship), and no EB or champion engagement exists to validate Zycus strategic value.

identify_pain.narrative: Nidec has articulated operational and strategic procurement pain across the evaluation, evidenced by the comprehensive 8-product scope requested (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Certinal, iRisk, Merlin Intake per Products__c) and the RFP requirements for operational-improvement case studies with specific KPIs (supplier onboarding time, sourcing automation, cycle-time reduction metrics per Next_Step__c). The pain points map to: (1) manual procurement processes and inefficiency (eProcurement, eInvoicing in scope), (2) supplier management gaps and onboarding delays (iSupplier, supplier onboarding KPI requested), (3) tail-spend visibility and control (AI customer success cases for tail-spend analytics requested per Next_Step__c), (4) sourcing automation needs (iSource, sourcing automation KPI requested), (5) contract lifecycle management (iContract), (6) risk visibility (iRisk), and (7) intake/requisition workflow (Merlin Intake, AI-powered). The RFP also requested AI use-case examples (tail-spend analytics, autonomous negotiating agent per Next_Step__c), signaling strategic appetite for AI-driven procurement transformation aligned with Merlin differentiation. However, the pain has NOT been quantified: no cost of the current-state inefficiency, no savings/ROI target, no headcount reduction goal, and no cycle-time improvement metric has been stated by the buyer (Manabu or any other stakeholder) across the 97-day cycle (created 26 Mar, last activity 1 Jul per SF authoritative facts). The pain owner is procurement-function-led (Manabu Serizawa, Procurement Officer per Contact Role, Decision Maker), but no C-level sponsor (CFO, exec-level Global CPO) has validated the strategic priority or quantified the business impact, a gap that lowers Zycus ability to anchor the pricing justification and secure executive buy-in before the shortlist-to-2 decision.

paper_process.narrative: The contracting and implementation mechanics are partially mapped. The buyer timeline in Next_Step__c states that contract negotiations and commercial terms alignment will begin after the vendor selection notification (late July to early August), indicating MSA/SOW/legal redlining is sequenced post-selection rather than in parallel during evaluation. No InfoSec review, ERP/systems integration deep-dive, or legal approval gate has been mentioned by the buyer across the RFI (31 Mar), Q&A (20 Apr), presentation (6 Jun), or RFP (30 Jun) milestones per Next_Step__c, a potential blind spot given the $550k investment and 8-product scope including Merlin Intake (Products__c). The SI partner role is unresolved and blocks implementation scoping: KPMG rejected Zycus partnership (aligned with the relevant stakeholder per Next_Step__c 31 Mar), Zycus is investigating TCS as alternative (Next_Step__c 31 Mar) but no SI has been locked in 90 days (created 26 Mar, last activity 1 Jul per SF authoritative facts), while the Coupa-backed finalist has KPMG as SI, creating a structural advantage on China pilot deployment, change management, and local support. The absence of a committed SI risks Nidec perception that Zycus cannot deliver the China-region rollout, a critical paper-process gap that must resolve before the shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August. No current-contract expiration date or forcing function is documented (Current_Contract_Expiration__c not provided in SOQL reads), so urgency is driven by Nidec's internal timeline rather than an external deadline.

decision_process.narrative: Nidec is running a formal, EY-advised, multi-gate procurement process with a clear timeline and milestones documented in Next_Step__c. The process map: (1) 31 Mar: RFI submission with pricing estimate, 4-vendor evaluation initiated (EY doing advisory work for Nidec Japan per Next_Step__c); (2) 13 Apr: shortlist discussions began, Q&A scheduled for 20-24 Apr; (3) 6 Jun: global presentation to stakeholders in Japan, Poland, US, China, shortlisted to 3 vendors; (4) 30 Jun: RFP response submission deadline (Zycus submitted on time per Next_Step__c 31st); (5) Early to mid July: initial evaluation and document review (current phase as of 1 Jul); (6) Late July: final presentation for shortlisted vendors that pass initial evaluation; (7) Late July to early August: final evaluation and vendor selection, notification of selection results; (8) After selection notification: contract negotiations and commercial terms alignment (Next_Step__c buyer timeline). The approval chain has NOT been disclosed: Manabu Serizawa (Decision Maker per Contact Role) requested pricing justification on 20 Apr, signaling he must defend upward, but the approver (CFO, exec-level Global CPO, procurement committee chair) has not been named or engaged. Izabela Smulska Kowalczyk (Global CPO per Next_Step__c 6 Jun) is connected but has not participated in any evaluation gate. The process is transparent on milestones but opaque on approval authority and whether the Economic Buyer validates before or after the shortlist-to-2 decision, a critical sequencing gap that affects Zycus leverage to validate the ROI case and secure exec sponsorship before the final gate.

decision_criteria.narrative: Nidec has run a structured, multi-gate evaluation process (RFI 31 Mar with 4 vendors, Q&A 20-24 Apr, global presentation 6 Jun shortlisting to 3, RFP due 30 Jun with document review in early-mid July, final presentation late July for shortlisted vendors per Next_Step__c buyer timeline), signaling formal decision criteria are in play. The RFP explicitly requested (1) detailed pricing explanation with transparent inclusion list (middleware, e-signature tool) and extended discount, (2) AI customer success cases (tail-spend analytics, autonomous negotiating agent examples), (3) list of global customers using Zycus in China (16 customers with network/VPN performance details), and (4) 5-6 operational-improvement case studies with specific KPIs (supplier onboarding time, sourcing automation, cycle-time reduction metrics) per Next_Step__c. These requirements map to evaluation dimensions: pricing competitiveness (Manabu flagged Zycus pricing as significantly higher than competitors on 20 Apr Q&A per Next_Step__c 13 Apr), AI differentiation (Merlin Intake in scope per Products__c), China deployment feasibility (global rollout with China pilot, 16 China customers requested), and operational ROI proof (KPI-anchored case studies). However, the relative weighting of these criteria, the scoring model, and whether Nidec prioritizes lowest-cost or best-value have NOT been disclosed by the buyer or EY advisory team. The Coupa-backed finalist (with KPMG as SI per Next_Step__c 31 Mar) may have preferred-partner positioning or incumbent-adjacent leverage, but no competitive intelligence has been shared to help Zycus understand the second finalist's strengths or Nidec's true decision priorities. Confirmed criteria exist but strategic blind spots remain.

bullets.full: Losing on capability fit — Nidec has confirmed AI appetite: Merlin Intake is in the 8-product scope (Products__c), and the RFP explicitly requested AI customer success cases (tail-spend analytics, autonomous negotiating agent examples per Next_Step__c). This signals strategic interest in AI-driven procurement transformation aligned with Zycus Merlin differentiation (AI-powered intake/requisition workflow, autonomous negotiation, tail-spend visibility and analytics). However, the AI business case has NOT been quantified by the buyer (no cost savings, headcount reduction, or cycle-time improvement target tied to AI capabilities), and no Economic Buyer or C-level sponsor has validated the AI investment rationale across the 97-day cycle (created 26 Mar, last activity 1 Jul per SF authoritative facts). The AI appetite is confirmed (AI use-cases explicitly requested), but the commercial validation is missing (no ROI case, no exec sign-off). Tier: AI Curious with structural fit but incomplete business-case validation. To elevate to AI Hungry: build quantified AI ROI case and present to Economic Buyer (Izabela or CFO) to validate AI business impact before shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Nidec has confirmed AI appetite: Merlin Intake is in the 8-product scope (Products__c), and the RFP explicitly requested AI customer success cases (tail-spend analytics, autonomous negotiating agent examples per Next_Step__c). This signals strategic interest in AI-driven procurement transformation aligned with Zycus Merlin differentiation (AI-powered intake/requisition workflow, autonomous negotiation, tail-spend visibility and analytics). However, the AI business case has NOT been quantified by the buyer (no cost savings, headcount reduction, or cycle-time improvement target tied to AI capabilities), and no Economic Buyer or C-level sponsor has validated the AI investment rationale across the 97-day cycle (created 26 Mar, last activity 1 Jul per SF authoritative facts). The AI appetite is confirmed (AI use-cases explicitly requested), but the commercial validation is missing (no ROI case, no exec sign-off). Tier: AI Curious with structural fit but incomplete business-case validation. To elevate to AI Hungry: build quantified AI ROI case and present to Economic Buyer (Izabela or CFO) to validate AI business impact before shortlist-to-2 decision in late July to early August.

hard.ais_why:
* The customer actively evaluates AI as a criterion: a formal RFP question asked "what are AI elements and what modules of your service are they used?" and Rocio Hinojosa "expressed a desire to understand the available AI solutions that have already been developed from a procurement perspective."
* Concrete AI benchmarking: the customer requested "AI success cases, including tail-spend analytics and autonomous negotiation examples, to benchmark platform capabilities," and discussion covered Zycus's agentic AI for sourcing/negotiation and its Anthropic Claude-based architecture.
* It reads as diligent RFP evaluation rather than excitement - this EY-led, multi-vendor process is also weighing pricing/discounts, China support and references on a $500K deal at 25%; AI is one criterion among many.

gaps: No Avoma calls discovered via opp-id or account-id pull for Nidec Corporation (Account.Id from Q1) or Opportunity 006P700000VnWhf in the evaluation period (created 26 Mar 2026, last activity 1 Jul 2026). MEDDPICC synthesis and competitive intelligence rely entirely on Salesforce fields (Next_Step__c, Contact Roles, Products__c) and cannot be cross-validated or enriched with buyer-voice quotes from discovery, Q&A, presentation, or RFP calls. This is a genuine knowledge gap: no call transcripts exist to surface buyer pain articulation, competitive positioning statements, champion advocacy behaviors, Economic Buyer validation conversations, or decision criteria details beyond what is logged in Next_Step__c text entries.

No Qualified_Submission_Date__c, Formal_Eval_Submission_Date__c, or Shortlisted_Submission_Date__c values available in SOQL Q2 read to compute precise time-in-stage for Formal Evaluation or Shortlisted stages. Time-in-stage math relies on CreatedDate 2026-03-26 as proxy, which may understate true stage duration if the opportunity existed in an earlier stage before Formal Evaluation entry. This limits verdict math precision but does not affect the directional read (forward momentum visible, no slips).

No AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, or AIS_Why__c values available in SOQL Q2 read. AI appetite is confirmed through Products__c (Merlin Intake in scope) and Next_Step__c (AI customer success cases requested in RFP), but no AIS field exists to provide a scored AI excitement baseline or to flag whether Merlin positioning is under/over-rated vs the buyer's stated AI appetite. This is a hygiene gap (knowledge exists in other fields) rather than a knowledge gap.

Second finalist identity unconfirmed: Next_Step__c states Zycus shortlisted to 3 (from 4) as of 6 Jun, with Coupa-backed finalist (KPMG as SI) identified as primary competitor per 31 Mar entry, but the second finalist's name has not been disclosed by Nidec or EY across any Salesforce field, task, or Next_Step__c entry. This is a genuine knowledge gap that limits competitive positioning strategy and is recorded as a vulnerability and a recommended move (request competitive intelligence from EY or Manabu).

No Current_Contract_Expiration__c value available in SOQL Q3 read. No forcing function or urgency driver (current contract end date, renewal deadline) is documented beyond Nidec's internal buyer timeline (vendor selection late July to early August per Next_Step__c). This limits the ability to assess whether the close date 2026-12-26 is anchored to an external deadline or purely internal planning. This is a hygiene gap (urgency is visible in buyer timeline) rather than a knowledge gap.
